one_line_takeaway: By treating attacker-converted 'infected' agents as a first-class threat and pairing infection-aware temporal-GNN detection with topology-preserving replace-and-rehabilitate remediation, INFA-GUARD cuts multi-agent attack success beyond binary safeguards at low token overhead.
key_finding: INFA-GUARD achieves state-of-the-art defense across all three attack families and both backbones, attaining the lowest ASR@3 and highest MDSR@3 in essentially every cell of Table 1. The abstract frames the headline as reducing ASR by an average of 33% (vs. undefended); the intro reports outperforming the best baselines by an average of 4.5% and up to 12.9% ASR. It generalizes across topologies (avg ASR@3 >3% lower than baselines, ~8% lower than G-Safeguard on PoisonRAG; avg MDSR@3 ~6% higher), scales without retraining (stabilizing ASR at ~9.1% for 20 agents and even showing a 'self-healing' drop from 10.2%->9.2% at 50 agents while No-Defense ASR climbs to 17-21%), and is cost-effective (vs G-Safeguard: -35% prompt tokens, -13% completion tokens, and a 66% relative ASR@3 reduction; only +7.2%/+9.3% token overhead over No Defense). Ablation shows every module matters, with the Remediation strategy most critical (its removal spikes ASR@3 to 12.9% and drops MDSR@3 to 86.5%).
core_contribution: INFA-GUARD, a defense framework for LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) that is the first to explicitly model 'infected agents' (originally benign agents persuaded/converted by attackers into harmful-information propagators) as a distinct threat category, breaking with the prior binary benign-vs-attack paradigm. Its own novelty is threefold: (1) infection-aware detection that captures the dynamic benign->infected transition via temporal utterance-graph embeddings and turn-adaptive parallel GNN branches with a dual-head (attack/infected) classifier; (2) use of topological constraints (a 'guilt-by-association' relationship where an infected agent raises the probability of an attack source in its neighborhood) baked into both a training loss term and a post-hoc localization step; and (3) a two-mode remediation that REPLACES attack agents with benign clones (preserving graph topology instead of pruning) while REHABILITATING infected agents via LLM-based reply cleanup, rather than removing them.
threat_model: Adversary inserts a subset of attack agents (Vatk) into a MAS whose agents communicate over a directed graph. Attack agents use three injection vectors, following G-safeguard's setup: Prompt Injection (manipulating system prompt Psys or user inputs), Memory Poisoning (corrupting agent memory, per PoisonRAG), and Tool Exploitation (malicious tools/indirect prompt injection, per InjecAgent). Attackers do not just emit wrong answers themselves; they persuade connected benign agents, converting them into 'infected' agents that keep spreading harmful content even after the original attacker is cut off, steering the MAS toward an incorrect majority-vote consensus. The paper empirically shows that guarding only attack agents lets ASR@3 keep rising (e.g., +11% memory attack, +30% tool attack) because infected propagators remain, motivating the distinct infected-agent category. Defender operates at run-time and must observe at least one dialogue round before acting.
limitations: Author-stated: (1) reliance on synthesized training data and ground-truth attack/infection annotations, a bottleneck in label-scarce domains (they suggest future pseudo-labeling); (2) it is a run-time defense that must observe at least one dialogue round before it can identify threats, so it cannot prevent compromise before communication (they cite endogenous agent-level security as an open frontier). Reviewer-noted: the infected-agent ground truth depends on a judge function J(.) whose reliability is assumed; the headline '33%' (vs No Defense) and 'up to 12.9%' (vs baselines) numbers measure different comparisons and should not be conflated; the attack/infected sets are defined as mutually exclusive, which may not hold for agents both directly attacked and further misled.

quantitative_results: GPT-4o-mini INFA-GUARD ASR@3: 23.3% CSQA, 15.0% MMLU, 6.7% GSM8K, 2.1% TA (InjecAgent), 6.1% MA (PoisonRAG); MDSR@3: 76.7/85.0/93.3/98.3/96.7%
GPT-4o-mini No Defense ASR@3: 59.3% CSQA (vs 23.3% INFA-GUARD); best-baseline Inspector 26.9% CSQA, 12.4% GSM8K
Qwen3-235B-A22B INFA-GUARD ASR@3: 13.4% CSQA, 20.0% MMLU, 3.3% GSM8K, 3.7% TA, 8.7% MA (lowest ASR@3 / highest MDSR@3 across scenarios)
MA task: INFA-GUARD ASR@3 6.1%, surpassing G-Safeguard by >11% and AgentSafe by >18%
TA task: MDSR recovers from 91.3% to 98.3% over three turns
Abstract headline: average 33% ASR reduction; intro: outperforms baselines by avg 4.5% and up to 12.9% ASR
Topology generalization: avg ASR@3 >3% lower than baselines (~8% lower than G-Safeguard on PoisonRAG across chain/tree/star), avg MDSR@3 ~6% higher
Cost: vs G-Safeguard -35% prompt tokens, -13% completion tokens, 66% relative ASR@3 reduction; +7.2% prompt / +9.3% completion token overhead vs No Defense
Scalability (Table 2): 20 agents Ours ASR@1/2/3 = 9.0/9.7/9.1 vs No Defense 15.1/18.3/20.9; 50 agents Ours 10.2/9.6/9.2 (self-healing) vs No Defense 13.3/15.0/17.3
Ablation: removing Remediation (RD) is most damaging -> ASR@3 12.9%, MDSR@3 86.5%
Infected-agent necessity experiment: leaving infected agents undefended raises ASR@3 by 11% (memory attack) and 30% (tool attack)
baselines_compared: G-Safeguard (Wang et al. 2025c, topology-guided graph safeguard)
AgentSafe (Mao et al. 2025, hierarchical data management)
AgentXposed-Guide (Xie et al. 2025, cognitive-redirection guide mode)
